name: Trigonometric Equations — General Solutions
whatIsIt: A TRIGONOMETRIC EQUATION involves a trig ratio of an unknown angle, like sin x = 1/2. Because sine, cosine and tangent REPEAT (are periodic), such equations have INFINITELY many solutions. We write them all with a GENERAL SOLUTION: sinθ = sinα → θ = nπ + (−1)ⁿα; cosθ = cosα → θ = 2nπ ± α; tanθ = tanα → θ = nπ + α (n is any integer). The PRINCIPAL solutions are the ones in the first cycle, like x = 30° and 150° for sin x = 1/2.
whyNeeded: Waves, oscillations, circular motion and AC electricity repeat, so their equations have repeating solutions. Capturing them all with one general formula is essential in physics and is a core Class 11 NCERT and JEE Main topic.
realLifeUses: Finding all times a Ferris wheel seat is at a given height
When a swinging pendulum returns to the same angle
Times an AC voltage hits a set value each cycle
Angles where a sound or light wave repeats its value

vocabulary.meaning: A solution in the first cycle, e.g. 0 ≤ x < 2π (or 0° ≤ x < 360°).
A formula (with integer n) giving ALL solutions of the equation.
Repeating at regular intervals — why trig equations have infinitely many solutions.

story.title: The Ferris Wheel
story.text: Aarav rides a Ferris wheel and notices his seat is at the same height again and again. 'When exactly?' asks Didi. 'Well, sin x = 1/2 first at 30° and 150°,' says Aarav, 'but the wheel keeps turning, so it happens every full circle after that.' Didi nods: 'So one equation, but infinitely many answers — the GENERAL solution captures them all.'
story.extractedProblem: For sin x = 1/2, what are the two principal solutions in the first turn (0° to 360°)?

lines.label: sin x = 1/2 → x = 30°, 150°, then repeat every 360°

visual.caption: Solutions of sin x = 1/2 appear again and again along the angle axis (30°, 150°, 390°, …) — infinitely many, captured by one general formula.

lines.label: sin θ = sin α → θ = nπ + (−1)ⁿ α
cos θ = cos α → θ = 2nπ ± α
tan θ = tan α → θ = nπ + α

standardMethod.summary: Reduce the equation to sinθ = sinα, cosθ = cosα, or tanθ = tanα by finding a known angle α. Then write the matching general solution and, if asked, list the principal solutions in the first cycle.
steps: Isolate the trig ratio, e.g. sin x = 1/2.
Find a reference angle α whose ratio matches (here α = 30° = π/6).
Write the general solution using the correct family: sine → nπ + (−1)ⁿα, cosine → 2nπ ± α, tangent → nπ + α.
For principal solutions, list the values in one full cycle (0 to 2π).

workedExamples.problem: Give the principal solutions of sin x = 1/2 in 0° ≤ x < 360°.
steps: Sine is also positive in the second quadrant: 180° − 30° = 150°.

workedExamples.problem: What is the smallest positive angle (in degrees) with sin x = 1?
steps: That is the smallest positive solution.

commonMistakes.mistake: Giving only one (or only the principal) solution and forgetting the rest.
commonMistakes.fix: Trig equations have INFINITELY many solutions. Use the general solution (with integer n) unless only principal values are asked.

commonMistakes.mistake: Using the wrong general formula (e.g. the cosine form for a sine equation).
commonMistakes.fix: Match the ratio: sine → nπ + (−1)ⁿα, cosine → 2nπ ± α, tangent → nπ + α.

easy.q: The principal solutions of sin x = 1/2 in [0°, 360°) are…
hintLadder: Sine positive in Q1 and Q2.

easy.q: How many solutions does sin x = 1/2 have in total?

medium.q: General solution of sin θ = sin α is…
General solution of cos θ = cos α is…

challenge.q: General solution of tan θ = tan α is…
Smallest positive angle (degrees) with sin x = 1?

questions.q: General solution of sin θ = sin α:
Smallest positive angle (degrees) with cos x = 0?
General solution of cos θ = cos α:

teachBackPrompt: Explain to a grown-up why sin x = 1/2 has more than one answer. Show the two principal solutions (30° and 150°) and describe how, because the wheel keeps turning, the same value returns every full circle — that's the general solution.

revisionCard.summary: Trig equations are periodic ⇒ infinitely many solutions. General solutions: sinθ = sinα → θ = nπ + (−1)ⁿα; cosθ = cosα → θ = 2nπ ± α; tanθ = tanα → θ = nπ + α. Principal solutions are those in one cycle (e.g. 30°, 150° for sin x = 1/2).

rememberIt.unpack: Periodic ⇒ infinitely many answers. Principal = first cycle; general = all of them.

studentQuestions.a: Trig functions repeat every cycle, so if one angle works, every angle a full turn away works too — infinitely many.
Principal solutions are the specific ones in the first cycle (e.g. 30°, 150°); the general solution is the formula that lists ALL of them.

formulas.name: Sine equation
formulas.whenToUse: Any equation reduced to a sine value.
